# Transcrição: 02-Understanding Industry Trends- Who Works With Me and Why

- **Fonte:** 02-Understanding Industry Trends- Who Works With Me and Why
- **Duração:** 18:57
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Okay, before we move on, I want you to think a little bit about the ways in which storytelling can help a variety of aspects of your business. Likely you have come here with one singular goal in mind. Maybe you've decided that storytelling can dramatically improve your marketing department. Maybe you're getting ready to pitch to investors and you've decided storytelling is going to be important for you. Maybe you have a sales team that really needs to expand their storytelling skills, whatever the reason, fantastic. But what I want you to do is to begin thinking already about the ways in which storytelling can influence every part of your business. What usually happens to me is something like this. I parachute into the marketing department. I begin working with the team and we begin developing marketing plans and decks and things like that. But eventually someone in that department or maybe a leader in the company realizes that the things I'm doing for the marketing department can also help sales. Or I can start working on keynotes with the CEO for an upcoming talk that they're going to be giving. Eventually, I sort of find myself in many parts of a business. But the problem with that is the word eventually. I hate the word eventually because eventually means you've lost money. It means you've wasted time. It means you've wasted resources. It means that I should have been there already. That storytelling should have already been infused into all parts of this business. I want you to start thinking about that now. I'm going to give you examples of ways that I've worked with businesses and particularly aspects of those businesses now so that you can take whatever singular purpose you have and continue moving forward with that purpose, but expand on the possibilities so that you can begin to see where storytelling can really make a profound difference in your entire business. So marketing, for example. I spend a great deal of time in the marketing department of many companies helping them infuse storytelling into everything they do. And that can be as small as when a customer calls you, what is the story that that customer receives? I've worked with companies who quite literally the story that the customer receives is based upon who answers the phone, which is to say that every time a customer calls, they receive a different story based upon whoever picked up the phone at that particular time. And so with those companies, what we do is we fashion a story, a real story so that every single time a customer calls you, they get a story and they get the right story and everyone is trained to tell that story in a highly effective way. Or maybe you're working on marketing decks and you need help with that. Oftentimes when I'm working with folks who are getting ready to pitch a product or a service and we're looking at a deck, I have to talk about the ways in which the first slide of that deck should be related in some way to the last slide of that deck, which for a lot of clients, it blows their mind. Or the idea that as you're about to flip from one slide to another, have you given your audience a reason to want that flip to happen?

## [03:02 - 06:03]

Have you given them a reason to actually want to see the next slide in the deck? Quite often clients don't. They make this odd assumption that people want to hear what they have to say. Customers operate on the principle that nobody wants to hear anything we have to say. And therefore the onus is always on us. We assume there are audiences who are not interested in anything that we have to offer. So we have to aggressively, strategically, tactically give them reasons to be excited about the next sentence we're going to say. With a marketing deck, it's exactly the same thing. You have to give your audience a reason to want to see the next slide. You can't just assume that the client you're pitching or the investors you're pitching or the customer you're trying to win over actually wants to see or hear what you have to offer. Storytelling will give you that ability to get people excited about what you're saying. Storytelling can also do wonders for your sales department. Sales departments tend to be filled with excellent communicators who can describe products and services in extraordinary ways. What they often struggle with is actually making deep and profound connections with the customers and the clients that they're trying to reach. They do a great job with the what, but they don't do such a good job with who they are as a person. I took a plane ride recently. It was a six hour flight and as I sat down next to the woman who I'd be sitting with for the next six hours, I noticed that she was reading a book, a book that I had read before so I mentioned that to her. She said, oh, did you like the book? And I said, yes, actually my wife and I both read the book. That was said purposefully. I was about to sit next to a woman for the next six hours and I wanted her to know I have a wife. I wanted her to know that I was not the kind of person who was talking to her because I wanted to hit on her because I thought she was attractive. I was talking about the book and not her. She said, oh, did your wife like the book? And I said, actually my wife has impeccable taste. Everything she likes is something that I typically like, which was also strategic. And I was letting her know not only do I have a wife, but I greatly respect and I love my wife. Again, I wanted to send a message to this woman that I was a safe person, that I was the kind of person you might want to sit next to on a flight. And so the plane took off. We were flying for a while. I noticed that she's a very fast reader. She already gotten through 100 pages in a short period of time. So I mentioned to her, wow, you're a fast reader, a compliment. And she said, oh, yeah, I'm very fast. Are you quick? And I said to her, well, when my children aren't bothering me or my cat isn't sitting on my lap, I can be really fast. But distractions kill me. Again, sending messages to her that I'm also a father and I have kids and I'm a cat owner. All of that was said to let this woman know that I am a person who she can trust. More importantly, who she can know. She said, oh, I have a cat and told me the cat's name. I told her my cat's name was Pluto.

## [06:03 - 09:07]

And then I told her a story about Pluto. A heart felt amusing story. It took about two minutes. At that point, this woman and I were best friends. We spoke for almost the entire flight. She put her book away and we chatted the entire time. By the time we landed, we had exchanged email addresses and I will probably be doing business with that woman at some point. My point here is that when we tell stories about our lives and we know what to say, we understand what people want to hear and we know the kinds of things that will sort of make us trusted, vulnerable, authentic people. When we can do those things, we can find our ways into the hearts and minds of the people we're with. Our clients, our customers, a woman sitting on a plane. That's what salespeople need to learn to do. When you can get someone to like you, simply based upon a few things that you say, strategically and tactically, tell a good story, you can sell almost anything. I could have sold almost anything to that woman by the end of the flight and she would have bought it. Because she liked me. Because she found me interesting. Because she felt like she was connected to me. Salespeople can do this and the results can be extraordinary. Marketing is another department where storytelling can make an enormous difference. I spend a lot of time with advertising companies, looking at their campaigns, looking for small ways that storytelling can make a difference. I was recently working with a company on a car commercial. In that commercial, there was a family of four in the car. As I watched the commercial, I asked the people making it, I said, tell me about the two kids in the back seat, the boy and the girl. They said, well, it's a son and a daughter. And I said, well, what are their names? And they hadn't assigned these characters names. I said, tell me about the son. They said, well, he's the son. I said, tell me about the daughter. They said, well, she's the daughter. I said, do they have names? They had not assigned names. I said, can you tell me anything about the two children sitting in the back seat of this car? They could not. Storytellers understand that characters and stories have to be actual people or nobody will care about them. And so we did simple things in that commercial so that the boy and the girl, the son and the daughter in that commercial, were actual living people that perhaps someone watching the commercial might connect with. We made sure that the son and the daughter had certain things that were in opposition to each other because contrast drives storytelling. When a son and a daughter have things that are completely divergent from each other, your chances of connecting with an audience increase. These tiny things can make enormous differences when you're trying to advertise to audiences, when you're trying to connect with a large group of people. The more small, almost microscopic choices that you make, increase your chances of someone watching a commercial and going, I know that person on the screen. I understand that person. And now I feel more connected to the product or the service that you're offering.

## [09:07 - 12:07]

Leadership. Storytelling can make an enormous difference in the way that you communicate as a leader. You probably already know this. That's probably why you're watching this now. But take note of it because all of the people who are leading in your business should be using storytelling. The problem with leadership today, the problem with the way we communicate is that so often leaders are afraid to say the things that are hard to say. Most of the time, leaders are leading with how wonderful they are doing, how great they are, how fantastic their decisions have been. Those are all easy things to say. That's why they're forgettable things to say. No one remembers it when you brag. No one remembers it when you boast. They remember vulnerability. They remember when someone says something hard. They remember when someone says something that other people won't say. When you learn how to become a storyteller, when you can tell a good story, a story about vulnerability that can also be inspiring, that can make all the difference in the world. I worked with a CEO of a large hospital organization a few years ago. She was getting ready to give a keynote to about 5,000 of her employees and we were trying to figure out what story she should tell. The one that she settled on was the story about how her hospital had botched her husband's knee surgery. That a mistake had been made during the surgery that resulted in an additional six months of recovery and a second surgery in order to fix the first. She decided that was the story she was going to tell to her 5,000 employees. It's not the kind of story that most leaders are willing to tell. It took courage. It took a lot of gumption. It took inner fortitude, a willingness to say that we are not perfect. The results were extraordinary. She did a brilliant job telling the story and the next morning she called me. She told me that from her car to her office she was stopped by about a dozen people. People who had never spoken to her before. People who worked for her that she had never really met who either wanted to tell her how important her talk was or how they wanted to tell her something about her hospital that she had never heard before. Something she needed to know. She opened her heart and mind. She acknowledged vulnerability and imperfection and as a result her people loved her for it and she found out things that she needed to know. When leaders are courageous enough, smart enough, when they're skilled enough to tell a great story with vulnerability, wonderful things can happen. It's easy to tell great stories about yourself. Storytellers understand the best stories are not always the ones where we are the heroes. The best stories are the ones where we say the hard things and that makes all the difference. Culture. Storytelling can make an enormous difference in the culture of your company. I want to tell you about my very best storytelling client. He's a person that I meet with about once a month.

## [12:07 - 15:10]

He owns an enormous factory and several other businesses and he's looking for ways to take storytelling into his environment, into his business and make a difference. And so we sit down and for about an hour we talk about his life and we find a story in his life to work on. Not a story related to business, just a personal story about something going on in his life. And we craft it and develop it in the way that I develop stories to compete in moth story slams. He could take the stories that we work on, go stand on stages in New York and perform very well. Once that story is done, crafted and ready to go, that's when we start talking about business. The purpose of it is we want to take his personal stories and apply it to the things that he's doing in his workplace to improve the culture, to improve his connection to his employees. And so he tells me the story about his son, his son a Little League baseball player. In the championship game of the World Series of Little League, his son is up to bat. The bases are loaded at the bottom of the ninth. If his son gets a hit, his team wins the town championship. If his son strikes out, they lose. His son strikes out. He watches as his son drags the bat from home plate over to the dugout looking as dejected as a little kid can. My client's heart is broken. Fifteen minutes later, he finally catches up to his son. He sees his son smiling and running up the hill with his teammates, heading to the cars because they know there's ice cream in their future. And he can't believe it. He is still devastated over the strikeout and the loss. But his son has put it behind him already and is already moving on to better days. And craft that story and then my client says, how can we use this in the business? And here's what we decide. He has a sales team and sometimes that sales team does not land the big sale. They lose to another company. And so I ask him, what happens to the sales team when they lose that big deal? And he says, they're depressed. They get really down on themselves. They're really hard on themselves. And sometimes that can go on for quite a while. And so my client now uses that story when speaking to the sales team. It's a story about accepting the fact that you can't always win and being okay with the idea that we can be upset about losing, that that's a normal part of human life. But we can't be upset for very long because there's another hill to climb. There's another battle to win. There's another client to conquer. If we allow ourselves to sit and wallow and get down on ourselves for too long, we miss opportunities. My client's son's name is Dan. And he's turned that into what he calls a Dan moment in his business. He tells the sales team, you're allowed to have Dan moments. When you strike out to lose the big game, you should drag the bat back to the dugout.

## [15:10 - 18:16]

You should be upset. You should be sad. But then you pick yourself up and you go after the next one. Stories change the culture of his business. And every month we work on a story that helps to develop his culture, change his culture, improve his culture through storytelling, through the personal stories that he takes to the workplace to connect with his employees and teach them the things he wants them to know. Storytelling can change the culture of your business in extraordinary ways. Lastly retention. Storytelling can help you keep your people, which I know is important to you. When your employees feel like they are connected to the work they do and connected to the company for whom they work, they are far more likely to remain working for you. By encouraging your employees to tell stories, by giving them the opportunity to tell stories, and perhaps by giving them the training needed to tell a great story, they're going to feel like that the work they do is valued. And that more than anything will keep people working for you. During the pandemic I worked with a hospital. They had me work with the nurses who had been through more trauma than they had ever been in their lives. They had just seen awful things in the everyday work that they did. And so I sat down with those nurses to craft up stories. Stories about their experiences during the pandemic. Both triumphant stories and tragic stories. The purpose, at least in the hospital's mind, was to collect stories about the pandemic so that when the next pandemic came and it will come, we'll be better prepared. We'll have a record of what nurses reported about during this pandemic so that the next one nurses and doctors would feel better prepared. But the after effect or the unexpected effect of that work that I did, nurses were so appreciative of the hospital, of their bosses, and of me, for allowing them the opportunity to share their experiences with other people. They were so happy to feel like the work they had done was valuable enough to be spoken about, to be afforded the time and the opportunity to learn how to speak about these things in a way that was crafted and meaningful and important. We actually ended up putting on a show where the nurses told those stories to a virtual audience, a virtual audience of hundreds. And the feelings that they had after that show were extraordinary. Those are people who are going to stay working at that hospital because they know that their bosses at that hospital value them and value their work. When you give your employees the opportunity to tell stories about the things they do and you make those stories worth something, people are far more likely to want to continue working with you tomorrow. So before you move on to the next video, I want you to stop and think for a moment about all of the places in your business where storytelling can help you. You have probably come here with one idea in mind, one reason why you think storytelling

## [18:16 - 18:57]

will change your company in a meaningful way, and I'm sure you're correct. But take a moment now and think about all of the departments in your company. Think about every single person who works with you or who might be working with you in the future. And all the ways that teaching them to become better storytellers will help your business. Don't allow yourself to be the kind of person who eventually discovers that storytelling is valuable. Do it now so that you don't waste the time, the money, and the time you spend on your business. So that you don't waste the time, the money, and the resources that you so desperately need.
